danger_signals.visual_form: character distance and animation frame changes
danger_signals.meaning: threat of incoming attack; player must dodge or counter

danger_signals.visual_form: randomly thrown object by trainer character
danger_signals.meaning: resource spawning event; uncollected balls indicate opponent can activate special moves

danger_signals.visual_form: distinct attack animation performed after energy ball collection
danger_signals.meaning: high-damage attack incoming; player is in danger state

danger_signals.visual_form: round counter incrementing; speed increases after round 100
danger_signals.meaning: escalating difficulty; match becomes more dangerous over time

safety_signals.meaning: temporary safety window; time to recover or plan next move
player power-up; access to special move capabilities for offense

safety_signals.visual_form: wrestling ring ropes as environmental boundaries
safety_signals.meaning: tactical geography; safe zones for positioning and setup moves

faction_indicators.symbol_or_sigil: individual character design (one of eight playable wrestlers)
faction_indicators.enemy_visual_variants: opponent renders differently when controlled by AI vs. player 2
faction_indicators.environmental_markers: wrestling ring as neutral arena; no faction-specific environmental coloring documented

faction_indicators.symbol_or_sigil: trainer character model; source of energy balls
faction_indicators.environmental_markers: ringside position; neutral arbiter role indicated by placement outside ring

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: standard match pacing and animation speed
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: opponents follow baseline difficulty AI; 30:00 round timer

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: increased animation speed; visual indication of escalating intensity
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: match speed increases; round timer reduced to 10:00; AI aggression likely increases

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: speed level resets; timer returns to 30:00
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: difficulty loop resets; game can theoretically continue indefinitely via 8-bit variable overflow

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: energy object appears in ring space
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: either player can collect it; holder gains access to special move capabilities

environmental_storytelling.technique: wrestling ring as stage of competition
environmental_storytelling.example: ropes serve as both mechanic (shove-into-ropes moves) and narrative element (formal combat arena signals legitimate sport)
ringside trainer character visually establishes wrestling match framing; energy ball distribution reinforces sport/match legitimacy
game's capacity for 255 rounds + wraparound suggests endurance/marathon combat narrative; wrestlers must persist indefinitely

entity_behavioral_tells.behavior: can execute special move (B button activation); gains offensive advantage
limited to basic attacks (punch, jump, drop kick, clothesline, suplex); defensive positioning required
standard difficulty baseline; fixed round timer of 30:00
increased speed and likely aggression; reduced round timer to 10:00
randomly throws energy balls into ring; neutral arbiter role

notes: M.U.S.C.L.E. is a simplified wrestling fighting game with minimal narrative world-state. Danger and safety are primarily mechanical (attack animations, distance, special move availability) rather than environmental or political. No faction conflict or political situation is present—the game is purely competitive sport simulation. World-state progression is tied to round number and difficulty scaling rather than narrative events. The game's visual communication relies on character animation frames, opponent positioning, and energy ball spawning as primary information channels. The rebranding from Kinnikuman (manga-based Japanese original) to M.U.S.C.L.E. (Mattel toy tie-in) for North America means visual design prioritizes generic wrestler archetypes over faction/character-specific storytelling.
sources_used: Wikipedia: Tag Team Match: M.U.S.C.L.E. (primary source for gameplay mechanics, character count, mode structure, round system, and difficulty progression)
